title: Introduction to Algorithms
subtitle: The Definitive Textbook on the Design and Analysis of Algorithms (CLRS)
author: Thomas H. Cormen
Charles E. Leiserson
Ronald L. Rivest
Clifford Stein
themes: The growth of functions and asymptotic notation as the language of algorithm analysis
Divide-and-conquer as a master method for structuring recursive algorithms
Heapsort, quicksort, and the deep theory of comparison-based sorting
Hash tables and the randomization that makes them perform in expected O(1) time
Binary search trees augmented into balanced structures (red-black, B-trees)
Dynamic programming and the importance of optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems
Greedy algorithms and matroid theory for proving optimality
Graph algorithms from BFS and DFS to minimum spanning trees and shortest paths
Maximum flow, matching, and the primal-dual view of network optimization
NP-completeness as a boundary between tractable and intractable problems
Approximation algorithms and randomized algorithms for hard problems
awards: Best 1990 Professional and Scholarly Book in Computer Science and Data Processing, Association of American Publishers
Named the most cited publication in computer science (over 70,000 citations on the 3rd edition alone)
Over 1,000,000 copies sold across all editions as of 2022
Standard reference at virtually every top computer science program worldwide
ACM Karl V. Karlstrom Outstanding Educator Award (Leiserson, 2010)
ACM A.M. Turing Award (Rivest, 2002) for co-invention of RSA cryptography
IEEE Computer Society Taylor L. Booth Award (Leiserson, 2014) for worldwide CS education impact
ACM-IEEE CS Ken Kennedy Award (Leiserson, 2014) for enduring influence on parallel computing
authorKnownFor: Systolic arrays and the retiming method for VLSI
Co-inventing the RSA public-key cryptosystem (Turing Award, 2002)
RC2, RC4, RC5, and RC6 symmetric ciphers
MD2, MD4, MD5, and MD6 hash functions
Founding RSA Data Security, VeriSign, and Peppercoin
Algorithms for big data and scheduling
Former Director of the Columbia Data Science Institute
Discrete Mathematics for Computer Science (with Bogart and Drysdale)
authorBiography: Thomas H. Cormen (born 1956) is an American computer scientist, author, and politician. He earned his B.S.E. in electrical engineering and computer science from Princeton University in 1978, then his S.M. (1986) and Ph.D. (1993) from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. He spent his career as a Professor of Computer Science at Dartmouth College — and later as Chair of the department and director of the Dartmouth Institute for Writing and Rhetoric — specializing in algorithm engineering, parallel computing, and techniques for accelerating computation under high latency. He was named an ACM Distinguished Educator for his pedagogical impact. He is the lead author of the canonical textbook Introduction to Algorithms (CLRS), now in its fourth edition (2022), and the author of the more accessible Algorithms Unlocked (2013). He also produced Khan Academy's algorithms tutorials. Since 2022 he has served as a Democratic state representative in the New Hampshire House of Representatives for Grafton District 15.

Charles E. Leiserson (born November 10, 1953) is an American computer scientist and the Edwin Sibley Webster Professor of Computer Science and Engineering at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. He earned his B.S. in computer science and mathematics from Yale University in 1975 and his Ph.D. in computer science from Carnegie Mellon University in 1981, where his dissertation 'Area-Efficient VLSI Computation' won the inaugural ACM Doctoral Dissertation Award in 1982. He joined the MIT faculty in 1981 and went on to become Associate Director and Chief Operating Officer of the MIT Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (CSAIL) and head of the Supertech research group. His inventions include the fat-tree interconnection network used in the CM-5 supercomputer, the retiming method of digital-circuit optimization, systolic arrays, the concept of cache-oblivious algorithms, and the Cilk multithreaded programming language (whose work-stealing scheduler he proved optimal). He was director of system architecture at Akamai Technologies and founded Cilk Arts, acquired by Intel in 2009. He is a member of the National Academy of Engineering and a Fellow of ACM, AAAS, SIAM, and IEEE. He was named a Margaret MacVicar Faculty Fellow, MIT's highest recognition for undergraduate teaching.

Ronald L. Rivest (born May 6, 1947) is an American cryptographer and computer scientist, and an Institute Professor — the highest academic honor at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology. He earned his B.A. in mathematics from Yale University in 1969 and his Ph.D. in computer science from Stanford University in 1974 under Robert W. Floyd. He joined MIT's Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science in 1974, where he is a member of CSAIL's Theory of Computation Group and founder of its Cryptography and Information Security Group. He is best known as a co-inventor of the RSA public-key cryptosystem (with Adi Shamir and Len Adleman), for which the three shared the 2002 ACM A.M. Turing Award. He also invented the RC2, RC4, RC5, and RC6 symmetric ciphers and the MD2, MD4, MD5, and MD6 hash functions, and co-founded RSA Data Security, VeriSign, and Peppercoin. His other work spans machine learning, combinatorial optimization, election security, and contact tracing. He is a member of the National Academy of Engineering, a Fellow of ACM, AAAS, and IEEE, and an inductee of the National Inventors Hall of Fame.

Clifford Stein (born December 14, 1965) is an American computer scientist and the Wai T. Chang Professor of Industrial Engineering and Operations Research, and of Computer Science, at Columbia University. He earned his B.S.E. from Princeton University in 1987 and his S.M. (1989) and Ph.D. (1992) from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, where his doctoral work on approximation algorithms for multicommodity flow and shop scheduling problems was supervised by David Shmoys. Before joining Columbia, he spent nine years on the computer science faculty at Dartmouth College. His research spans the design and analysis of algorithms, combinatorial optimization, scheduling, algorithm engineering, network algorithms, and computational biology. He is a Fellow of the ACM, the recipient of an NSF CAREER Award, an Alfred P. Sloan Research Fellowship, and the Karen Wetterhahn Award for Distinguished Creative or Scholarly Achievement. He has served as chair of Columbia's Department of Industrial Engineering and Operations Research and as interim director of the Columbia Data Science Institute. He is also the co-author of Discrete Mathematics for Computer Scientists.
excerpt: Widely known as 'CLRS' after its four authors, Introduction to Algorithms is the definitive textbook on the design and analysis of algorithms. Spanning the analysis of growth functions, divide-and-conquer, sorting, hash tables, balanced search trees, dynamic programming, greedy algorithms, graph algorithms, NP-completeness, and approximation algorithms, it provides the rigorous mathematical foundation on which modern computer science is built and has been translated into more than a dozen languages.

relatedBooks.title: Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs
